experiences.intentions:
Player 1: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns; as a Good Servant on round 1, a standard optimal approach is to approve my own opening 2-player team absent evidence, since rejecting gives little information and risks unnecessary cycling. Approving reveals little beyond basic willingness to test an initial team, which does not significantly hurt Good.
Player 3: No concrete role information yet from round 1, so all players are initially uncertain. As a Good Servant on the first vote, approving an initial 2-player team is generally optimal to generate quest information; rejecting without evidence gives little value and risks unnecessary failed votes. My vote reveals willingness to gather information rather than baseless suspicion, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are evil. The proposed quest team [0,1] includes known evil player 0, so as Good I should reject to avoid sending an evil on quest 1. Rejecting also avoids giving evil an easy fail or forcing evil to pass for cover; the clear signal is that this team is bad from my perspective, though I must be careful not to be too obvious later. Here, reject is optimal.
Player 2: I am evil Assassin with teammate 0. Proposed team [0,1] includes one evil and excludes me. On quest 1, a 2-player team with only 0 can choose to pass to avoid early exposure while still building trust for evil. Approving a team with my teammate on it is generally good; rejecting could look odd and misses a chance for evil-controlled information shaping. My vote reveals little because approving early teams is common.
Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate; players 1, 3, and 4 are not confirmed beyond likely good. This is an early team vote on quest 1 with team [0,1], which includes me but not my teammate. As evil, approving gets an evil player onto the first quest and can create ambiguity later; on a 2-player quest I can choose to pass to avoid exposing myself while still gaining trust. Rejecting a team with myself on it is suspicious and gives up influence. Approving reveals little and helps evil more than hurt.

Player 1: Quest 1 team [0,1] succeeded, so both 0 and 1 look somewhat good, though one could still be evil hiding on an early 2-player quest. Adding 3 to make [0,1,3] is reasonable with limited evidence; rejecting gives little benefit this early. Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, so this proposed team [0,1,3] contains only one evil (me) and excludes 2. Player 2: I know player 0 is evil with me; players 1 and 3 are unknown but team [0,1] previously succeeded, so 1 may be trusted by good. Current phase is team voting on my proposed team [0,1,3]. As evil, getting 0 onto teams is good, and approving my own team helps pass a composition that includes one evil without immediately exposing both evils. My vote reveals little since leaders often approve their own teams; rejecting would look odd and risk losing a favorable team.
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are evil. The proposed team [0,1,3] includes known evil player 0, so from a good-team perspective this team is unsafe and should be rejected. Voting reject reveals little because any rational good player should oppose a team containing suspicious members, and as Merlin I should avoid approving evil-containing teams when possible.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I know 0 and 2 are evil, so the proposed team [1,3,4] excludes known evil players and is the strongest available 3-person team from my perspective. We are at quest 4 with evil already at 2 fails, so approving a clean team is critical for Good to stay alive. My approve vote is informative in a good way because it supports a team free of known evil and may help others align around the correct pool.
Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, and I am evil. Team [1,3,4] contains no known evil, so if approved it is likely an all-good team and would likely succeed, giving Good a second point. Since Evil already leads 2-1 on quest results, the optimal play is to reject strong-looking all-good teams when possible and try to get an evil player onto the quest later. My vote itself gives limited info; rejecting is consistent with an evil player wanting to block a clean team.
Player 1: I am Good (Servant). From prior quests: [0,1] succeeded, so 0 and 1 looked good then, but later [0,1,3] failed, implying at least one of 0 or 3 is Evil. Since [2,3] also failed, 3 is the strongest Evil suspect. That makes 0 likely Good. Team [1,3,4] includes highly suspicious 3, so as Good I should reject to avoid likely another fail. Rejecting also signals that I do not trust 3-based teams and preserves information by forcing a better proposal.
Player 3: As Good Servant, I know only from quest outcomes. Q1 [0,1] succeeded, so 0 and 1 are somewhat cleaner. Q2 [0,1,3] had exactly one fail, so one of {0,1,3} is evil. Q3 [2,3] had exactly one fail, so one of {2,3} is evil. Combining these, player 3 (me) is good, so player 2 must be evil from Q3, and then from Q2 one of {0,1} is evil. Team [1,3,4] excludes known evil 2 and includes me; since one of 0/1 is likely evil and 4 is untested, this team has a decent chance to avoid both evils and is worth approving. Rejecting gives less information and risks cycling. Approving reveals whether 1 or 4 is bad via the 4-person quest result.
Player 2: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Team [1,3,4] excludes both known evil players, so if approved it is very likely all-good and would succeed, bringing score to 2-2 and risking a comeback. As evil on team voting in quest 4 with evil already at 2 fails, optimal play is to reject good-looking teams, especially in round 1 where there is plenty of room to find or force a sabotage-capable team later. My reject is consistent with evil interests and reveals little, since skepticism of a 3-player team that excludes prior fail participants is plausible.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I know 0 and 2 are Evil, and this proposed team includes both of them plus me. With Evil already up 2-1, approving a team with two known Evil players is disastrous because they can guarantee a fail. As Merlin, I should reject obviously bad teams while avoiding overly explicit signaling; a reject here is natural for any Good player given the evidence from prior failed quests.
Player 0: I know player 2 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [0,2,4] includes both evil players, guaranteeing we can fail quest 4 if approved and win immediately. Previous fails on [0,1,3] and [2,3] create suspicion elsewhere, so approving this team is optimal for evil and not especially revealing since teams with me and 2 are already plausible. My vote should maximize chances this team goes through.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Known info: Quest 1 success on team [0,1,3] means at least those members could be good, though evil can pass. Quest 2 failed on [2,3], and since I am good, player 2 must be evil. Quest 3 team [1,3,4] was rejected; no new hard info. Now proposed team [0,2,4] includes confirmed-evil 2, so for Good optimal play is to reject. My reject also signals that I recognize 2 as bad from the two-person failed quest, which helps Good coordinate.
Player 2: I am Assassin with evil teammate 0. Proposed team [0,2,4] contains both evil players, which gives us strong control to fail quest 4 and likely win immediately with 3 fails. Publicly, 4 is uncertain but irrelevant since two evils are already on team. As evil, optimal play is to approve a team with both evils. My approve is consistent with wanting this strong team through; rejecting would risk a different team that may exclude one of us.
Player 1: I’m Good with limited info. Q1 [0,1,3] succeeded, so at most one of {0,1,3} could be evil if they chose to pass, but more likely those players are safer. Q2 [2,3] failed with one fail, so at least one of 2 or 3 is evil. Since 3 was also on the successful Q1, 2 is the stronger evil suspect. Proposed team [0,2,4] includes 2, the strongest suspect, and excludes me despite my known-good perspective. As Good on vote phase, optimal play is to reject suspicious teams and push for teams centered on the Q1 success pool. My reject signals that including 2 is not acceptable based on quest history, which helps Good more than approving a likely bad team.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Merlin and know 0 and 2 are evil. The proposed team [1,2,4] includes confirmed evil player 2, so it should be rejected. At team voting, optimal good play is to reject any team containing known evil. My vote may signal suspicion of 2, but rejecting is still necessary because approving likely hands evil the game on quest 4.
Player 0: I know player 2 is evil with me, so this proposed team [1,2,4] contains one guaranteed evil. Since evil already has 2 failed quests, our optimal play is to get this team approved because quest 4 only needs one fail in 5-player Avalon and would immediately secure the third failed quest for evil. Approving does not significantly expose me, since an evil player should support a team containing their partner that can win the game.
Player 3: We are Good Servant at team vote with score Good 1 / Evil 2, so one more fail loses. Prior quests: [2,3] failed with exactly 1 fail, so either 2 or 3 is evil (and I know I am good, so 2 is evil). Earlier [1,3,4] was rejected, giving no direct quest info. Proposed team [1,2,4] includes player 2, who is strongly implicated by the failed 2-player quest with me. Approving likely hands evil the game. Rejecting is optimal. My reject signals suspicion on 2, which helps good coordinate more than it helps evil.
Player 2: I know 0 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 team [likely 0,1] had a fail, and Quest 2 team [2,3] had a fail, so suspicion should be on 2 and also possibly 0/1. This proposed team [1,2,4] includes me and excludes 0, making it plausible enough to pass. Since evil already leads 2-1, getting onto quest 4 is ideal because if approved I can fail and end the game before assassination. Voting approve helps secure a team with an evil player on it and doesn't strongly expose extra info beyond my likely willingness to pass teams containing me.
Player 1: As a good Servant, I know only from quest outcomes and votes. Quest 1 [2,3] failed, so at least one of 2 or 3 is evil. Quest 2 [1,3,4] failed with me on it, and I am good, so at least one of 3 or 4 is evil. Therefore player 3 is highly likely evil (fits both fails), though the pair 2 and 4 evil is also possible. Proposed team [1,2,4] excludes the strongest evil suspect 3 and includes me, so it is one of the best available teams. On quest 4, good must stop evil from getting a third fail, but rejecting a plausible anti-3 team gives little benefit and risks worse future teams or auto-pass pressure. Approving reveals that I support the anti-3 configuration, which helps good coordination more than it hurts.
